chapter: Chapter: Electricity – Magnetic and Heating Effects

questions.question: Define electric current and explain how it flows in a conductor.
questions.answer: Electric current is the flow of electric charges through a conductor. When a battery is connected in a closed circuit, electrons move through the conductor causing current to flow.

questions.question: Explain the components of a simple electric circuit.
questions.answer: A simple electric circuit consists of a battery to provide energy, wires to carry current, a switch to control current and a device such as a bulb that uses electrical energy.

questions.question: Differentiate between open circuit and closed circuit.
questions.answer: In a closed circuit the path is complete and current flows, while in an open circuit the path is broken and current cannot flow.

questions.question: Explain the heating effect of electric current with examples.
questions.answer: When current flows through a conductor, electrical energy is converted into heat due to resistance. Appliances such as electric irons and heaters use this effect.

questions.question: Why do electric heaters use coils made of special materials?
questions.answer: Heating coils are made of materials with high resistance and high melting point so that they produce heat without melting.

questions.question: Explain the function of an electric fuse in a circuit.
questions.answer: An electric fuse protects a circuit by melting when excessive current flows, thereby breaking the circuit and preventing damage to appliances.

questions.question: Explain what happens when a short circuit occurs.
questions.answer: A short circuit occurs when wires of different potential touch each other, causing a sudden large current that may damage the circuit or cause overheating.

questions.question: Explain the magnetic effect of electric current.
questions.answer: The magnetic effect of electric current is the phenomenon in which a current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it.

questions.question: Describe the experiment that shows the magnetic effect of current.
questions.answer: When a compass is placed near a current-carrying wire, the needle deflects showing that current produces a magnetic field.

questions.question: Define an electromagnet and explain its construction.
questions.answer: An electromagnet is a temporary magnet made by passing electric current through a coil of wire wound around an iron core.

questions.question: Explain why an electromagnet is called a temporary magnet.
questions.answer: An electromagnet works only when current flows through the coil. When the current stops, the magnetism disappears.

questions.question: Explain two applications of electromagnets.
questions.answer: Electromagnets are used in electric bells and cranes used in scrap yards for lifting heavy iron objects.

questions.question: Explain how the number of coil turns affects an electromagnet.
questions.answer: Increasing the number of turns in the coil increases the strength of the electromagnet.

questions.question: Explain how electric bells use electromagnets.
questions.answer: An electric bell uses an electromagnet to attract a metal armature which strikes the bell repeatedly, producing sound.

questions.question: Explain why electric bells ring continuously when the switch is pressed.
questions.answer: The circuit repeatedly breaks and reconnects, causing the armature to move back and forth and strike the bell continuously.

questions.question: Explain why electricity is considered a useful form of energy.
questions.answer: Electricity can easily be converted into heat, light and mechanical energy, making it useful in many devices.

questions.question: Explain the role of switches in electric circuits.
questions.answer: Switches control the flow of current by opening or closing the circuit.

questions.question: Explain why copper wires are commonly used in electric circuits.
questions.answer: Copper is a good conductor of electricity and allows current to flow easily through it.

questions.question: Explain how excessive current can cause electrical hazards.
questions.answer: Too much current can cause wires to overheat, which may damage appliances or cause fires.

questions.question: Explain the importance of electrical safety in homes.
questions.answer: Electrical safety prevents accidents such as shocks, fires and damage to appliances.

questions.question: Explain how electricity produces both heat and magnetism.
questions.answer: Electric current flowing through a wire produces heat due to resistance and also produces a magnetic field around the wire.

questions.question: Explain the importance of electromagnets in modern technology.
questions.answer: Electromagnets are widely used in machines, electric bells, motors and cranes for lifting heavy metal objects.

questions.question: Explain why an electromagnet can be stronger than a permanent magnet.
questions.answer: The strength of an electromagnet can be increased by increasing current or number of coil turns.

questions.question: Explain how an electric circuit transfers energy.
questions.answer: Energy from the battery is transferred through the circuit to electrical devices where it is converted into other forms such as light or heat.

questions.question: Explain how electricity and magnetism are connected.
questions.answer: Electric current flowing through a conductor produces a magnetic field, showing that electricity and magnetism are closely related.
